1: megalēs ek tou naou legousēs tois hepta angelois: a loud voice from the temple commanding the seven angels. The voice from the temple (ek tou naou) — the divine command originates from the inaccessible sanctuary of Revelation 15:8. The seven bowls are poured at explicit divine command, not on the angels' initiative. The bowl judgments are the most direct expression of divine wrath in Revelation: no human or angelic mediating action, just the direct pouring of God's fury onto the earth.
2: helkos kakon kai ponēron: ugly and painful sores (helkos). The Greek word helkos is used in the LXX for the boils of the sixth Egyptian plague (Exodus 9:9-11: helkē phlyktidēs, sores of blisters) — the direct connection to the exodus plagues is explicit. The first bowl targets those who have the mark of the beast and worship its image — the judgment is specific and just: those who aligned themselves with the beast system receive the first plague. The Egyptian plagues distinguished between Israel and Egypt (Exodus 8:22-23); the bowls distinguish between beast-worshippers and the sealed.
3: hōs nekrou: like blood of a dead person — nekros (corpse) modifies the blood: not fresh arterial blood but the thick, dark, clotted blood of a corpse. Echoes the first Egyptian plague (Exodus 7:17-21) but amplified: not just one river but the entire sea. Pasa psychē zōēs apethanen: every living soul (psychē zōēs — the Hebraic phrase nefesh chayyah from Genesis 1:20-21, 24; Revelation 2:7) died. The universal mortality of marine life signals that the old creation's life-sustaining systems are failing under divine judgment.
4: egenonto haima: they became blood. The third bowl turns rivers and springs to blood — now the fresh water supply joins the salt water. The escalation from sea to rivers to springs makes the judgment total: no water source is unaffected. Taken together with the second bowl, the entire hydrological system of the planet becomes the instrument of judgment — the waters that sustain life become the blood that witnesses to death.
5: dikaios ei, ho ōn kai ho ēn, ho hosios: "You are just, the one who is and who was, the Holy One" — the angel of the waters affirms divine justice using the Revelation formula for God's eternal being (Revelation 1:4, 8; Revelation 4:8). Hosios (holy, set apart for God) applied to God himself — the only being who is intrinsically holy. Hoti tauta ekrinas: because you judged these things — the judgment is legally grounded (krinō), not arbitrary revenge. The angel of the waters (ho angelos tōn hydatōn) testifies from within the judgment he experiences: even the created order acknowledges the justice of its own transformation into blood.
6: hoti haima hagiōn kai prophētōn execheran kai haima autois dedōkas pein: "for they poured out the blood of saints and prophets, and you have given them blood to drink — they deserve it." Axios eisin: they are worthy/deserving — the same axios used of the Lamb's worthiness to receive worship (Revelation 5:12) now applied to the persecutors' worthiness to receive judgment. The lex talionis operates at cosmic scale: those who shed blood receive blood. The rivers-to-blood judgment is the precise inversion of their crime.
7: ēkousa tou thusiastēriou legontos: I heard the altar (thusiastērion) saying. The altar speaks — the altar that held the souls of the martyrs (Revelation 6:9-10) who cried "How long?" now responds. The altar's voice affirms: Nai, Kyrie ho theos ho Pantokratōr, alēthinai kai dikaiai hai kriseis sou (Yes, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are your judgments). The martyred souls have been answered: the God of justice has acted. The altar's response closes the loop opened in Revelation 6:9-10.
8: edothē autō kaumatisai tous anthrōpous en pyri: it was given to the sun to scorch people with fire. Edothē — the divine passive again: the fourth plague is divinely authorized. The sun, which sustains life, becomes the instrument of judgment — creation's benevolent functions inverted. Kauma mega: intense heat — a judgment that cannot be hidden from or escaped; it falls on all exposed to it.
9: eblasphēmēsan to onoma tou theou tou echontos tēn exousian epi tas plēgas tautas kai ou metenoesan dounai autō doxan: they blasphemed the name of God who has authority over these plagues, and they refused to repent to give him glory. The response to judgment is the diagnostic: repentance or hardening. The plague-recipients know the source of the plagues (tou echontos tēn exousian = God) and choose blasphemy over worship. This is Pharaoh's hardening under the Egyptian plagues (Exodus 7:3; Revelation 9:12; Revelation 10:1) reproduced at cosmic scale — the final plagues confirm the final hardening of those who will not repent.
10: ton thronon tou thēriou: the throne of the beast — the political power center attacked directly. Egeneto hē basileia autou eskotōmenē: its kingdom was plunged into darkness (skotizō). The ninth Egyptian plague was three days of darkness (Exodus 10:21-23); here the darkness falls on the beast's entire kingdom. Emasōnto tas glōssas autōn ek tou ponou: they gnawed their tongues from the pain — an image of anguish with no remedy. The darkness does not produce reflection but intensified suffering.
11: eblasphēmēsan ton theon tou ouranou ek tōn ponōn autōn kai ek tōn helkōn autōn kai ou metenoesan ek tōn ergōn autōn: they blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pain and sores, and refused to repent of what they had done. The refusal to repent is repeated (cf. Revelation 16:9) as the defining response. Ek tōn ergōn autōn: from their works — repentance would require acknowledging that their works were wrong. The beast-worshippers are defined by the works they have chosen; repentance would mean abandoning the entire framework of identity they have built around the beast-cult. Final judgment hardens rather than softens.
12: ton potamon ton megan ton Euphratēn: the great river Euphrates. The drying of the Euphrates to prepare the way for the kings from the east echoes multiple OT passages: Isaiah 41:2, 25 (the one from the east whom YHWH has raised up); Isaiah 44:28-45:1 (Cyrus from the east who dries the rivers); the crossing of the Red Sea (Exod 14) and Jordan (Josh 3-4). Hina hetoimasthē hē hodos tōn basileōn tōn apo anatolēs hēliou: the way is prepared for the kings from the rising of the sun. The irony: the drying of the Euphrates (the boundary of the empire) allows the assault that will destroy Babylon.
13: pneumata tria akatharta homoia batrachois: three impure spirits like frogs. The frog plague is the second Egyptian plague (Exodus 8:1-15) — the unclean animal associated in Jewish thought with impurity and noise. The three frog-spirits emerge from the unholy trinity: the dragon, the beast, and the false prophet. This demonic triad parodies the Holy Trinity — as the Spirit proceeds from the Father and the Son, these spirits proceed from the dragon's unholy delegation. Ekporeuomena ek tou stomatos: proceeding from the mouths — from speech, propaganda, and deceptive proclamation rather than from power.
14: pneumata daimoniōn poiountas sēmeia: spirits of demons performing signs (sēmeia). The miraculous signs authenticate the false assembly call — just as the false prophet performed signs to compel beast-worship (Revelation 13:13-14). The target: tous basileis tēs oikoumenēs holēs (the kings of the whole inhabited world) — assembled for battle (polemon) on the great day of God Almighty (tēs hēmeras tēs megalēs tou theou tou Pantokratoros). The cosmic battle is not fought by human forces but assembled by demonic deception — the kings think they are gathering for conquest but are being gathered for their own destruction.
15: Idou erchomai hōs kleptēs: "Look, I come like a thief" — a parenthetical interruption of Christ in the narrative. The same image as 1 Thessalonians 5:2; 2 Peter 3:10; Matthew 24:43-44: the thief's coming is sudden and unannounced. Makarios ho grēgorōn kai tērōn ta himatia autou: blessed is the one who stays awake and keeps his garments — the sixth beatitude in Revelation. Keeping garments (himatia) = spiritual alertness and prepared readiness. The naked shame (gymnos) that follows is Adam's shame (Genesis 3:7) and the Laodiceans' (Revelation 3:18): spiritual unprepared exposure. The beatitude is addressed directly to readers in the churches: the battle of Armageddon is the background, the call to watchfulness is the pastoral word.
16: ton topon ton kaloumenon Ebraisti Harmagedōn: the place called in Hebrew Harmagedōn. The Greek transliterates Hebrew הַר מְגִדּוֹ (Har Megiddo) = Mount of Megiddo. Megiddo in the Valley of Jezreel (Esdraelon) was the site of decisive historical battles: Deborah and Barak defeated Sisera near Megiddo (Judges 5:19); the good king Josiah died at Megiddo (2 Kings 23:29). Zechariah 12:11 speaks of the great mourning of Megiddo as a figure for eschatological lamentation. Revelation uses the name as a symbolic assembly point — the place of decisive battle becomes the world-stage for the final conflict between the Lamb and the beast.
17: Gegonen: "It is done!" — the perfect of ginomai: it has happened and stands accomplished. The same announcement formula as 21:6 at the completion of the new creation, here marking the completion of the bowl judgments. The voice from the throne, from the temple (apo tou naou apo tou thronou) — the divine sanctuary is the source of both the command (Revelation 16:1) and this completion announcement. Apo tou thronou: from the throne where God and the Lamb are enthroned (Revelation 22:1, 3) — the completion of judgment is declared from the seat of sovereign authority.
18: seismos megas oios ouk egeneto aph' hou anthrōpos egeneto epi tēs gēs: an earthquake (seismos) such as has never occurred since humans were on the earth. The superlative earthquake — exceeding all previous seismic events in human history — marks the eschatological finality of the seventh bowl. Tēlikoutos seismos houtōs megas: so great an earthquake. The earthquake as divine theophany (Exodus 19:18; Psalms 68:8; Isaiah 29:6; Ezekiel 38:19-20) here reaches its ultimate expression — the entire geophysical order disrupted by the presence of God's final judgment.
19: hē polis hē megalē eis tria merē: the great city split into three parts. The great city (hē polis hē megalē) = Babylon/Rome (16:19 confirms: Babulōn hē megalē). The tripartite division signals total structural collapse — not damage but disintegration. Emnēsthē Babulōn hē megalē enōpion tou theou dounai autē to potērion tou oinou tou thymou tēs orgēs autou: God remembered Babylon — emnēsthē (remembered) is the divine memory that is coextensive with judgment: when God remembers an evil, he acts against it. The cup of wrath (potērion tou oinou tou thymou) that Babylon forced others to drink (Revelation 14:8; Revelation 17:2; Revelation 18:3) is now given to her.
20: pasa nēsos ephugen kai orē ouch heurethēsan: every island fled and the mountains could not be found. The disappearance of the geological features — islands and mountains — signals the dissolution of the physical order that has provided hiding places and boundaries. Compare Revelation 6:14 (every mountain and island was removed from its place) — the sixth seal previewed what the seventh bowl completes. The mountains and islands that the kings of the earth had retreated to for refuge (Revelation 6:15-16) are no longer available. The creation's landmarks are erased.
21: chalaza megalē hōs talentiaia katabainen ek tou ouranou epi tous anthrōpous: great hailstones, about a talent's weight (talentiaia), falling from heaven on people. A talent = approximately 75-130 pounds. The seventh Egyptian plague was hail (Exodus 9:23-25) — here magnified to catastrophic weight. Eblasphēmēsan hoi anthrōpoi ton theon ek tēs plēgēs tēs chalazēs: people blasphemed God because of the plague of hail. The final response to the final plague is still blasphemy — the hardening is complete. The bowl judgment cycle ends where it began (Revelation 16:9): with people who know the source of the plagues and curse rather than worship. No repentance occurs under the bowls; this is the judgment, not the call to repentance.
